title: Knowledge and the Fear of the Lord — Revelation and Reverence
reading:
The last pairing in Isaiah 11 — Knowledge and the Fear of the Lord — is also the most foundational. Proverbs 1:7 frames the entire wisdom tradition of Scripture on this relationship: "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge." You cannot have one rightly without the other.

Knowledge without the Fear of the Lord produces pride. The Fear of the Lord without Knowledge produces superstition. Together they produce transformation.

The Fall in Genesis 3 is the first and most devastating case study. Adam and Eve possessed real knowledge. They had walked with God, heard His voice, and received His command. But when they chose the fruit, they demonstrated that knowledge alone does not produce obedience. What was missing was not information. What was missing was reverence — the kind that would have caused them to treat God's word as sacred.

Contrast this with Moses at the burning bush. When God spoke, Moses removed his sandals and hid his face. He had encountered God's holiness and responded correctly. Over the following forty years, his knowledge of God grew deeper than any man's — "face to face, as a man speaks to his friend" (Exodus 33:11). The reverence did not diminish as the intimacy grew. It deepened. The more Moses knew God, the more he feared Him. The more he feared Him, the more God revealed.

King Josiah's response when the Book of the Law was rediscovered is one of Scripture's clearest demonstrations. When the scroll was read to him, Josiah tore his robes (2 Kings 22:11). He had received knowledge — the actual words of God. His immediate response was the Fear of the Lord: grief, repentance, action. He did not file the information away. He was undone by it. True knowledge, when the Fear of the Lord is present, always produces transformation.

Isaiah's vision in chapter 6 operates identically. He sees God. He immediately cries, "Woe is me, for I am ruined." Greater revelation produced greater reverence. And greater reverence positioned him for greater usefulness: "Here I am, send me."

Jesus said in John 17:3: "This is eternal life — that they know You, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom You have sent." Knowledge of God is not a means to an end. It is the end. And the Fear of the Lord is what keeps that knowledge from becoming mere religion.
reflection:
Is there an area of your life where you have received revelation from God that has not yet produced transformation? What would the Fear of the Lord look like in that area?

How has your reverence for God grown as your knowledge of Him has grown — or has it been growing? Where might reverence be lagging behind what you know?

What would it look like this week to respond to one thing you know about God with genuine reverence rather than simply acknowledging it intellectually?
abide: Lord, let my knowledge of You always produce reverence, not familiarity. I don't want to be someone who knows much and is changed little. Search me — where I have accumulated truth without allowing it to transform me, do the work that only You can do. Let every revelation of who You are deepen my fear of You, and let every increase in reverence open me to know You more. Let Knowledge and the Fear of the Lord grow together in me until I am fully Yours.
